questions.explanation: Please refer to your NCERT textbook for a detailed explanation of this concept.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two long straight wires are placed along x-axis and y-axis. They carry current I1 and I2 respectively. The equation of locus of zero magnetic induction in the magnetic field produced by them is
questions.explanation: B due to wire on x-axis = μ0I1/2πy (perpendicular to plane, outwards) B due to wire on y-axis = μ0I2/2πx (perpendicular to plane, inwards) For zero, they must be equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. So μ0I1/2πy = μ0I2/2πx ⇒ I1/y = I2/x ⇒ y = (I1/I2)x

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If B1, B2 and B3 are the magnetic field due to I1, I2 and I3, then in Ampere's circuital law ∮ B·dl = μ0 I, B is
questions.explanation: In ampere circuital law, on amperian loop B is due to all the current elements either inside or outside to the amperian loop.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two moles of an ideal gas are contained within a cylinder by a frictionless piston and are initially at temperature T. The pressure of the gas remains constant while it is heated and its volume doubles. If R is molar gas constant, the work done by the gas in increasing its volume is
questions.explanation: W = PΔV = P(2V - V) = PV = nRT = 3RT

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two moles of a gas at temperature T and volume V are heated to twice its volume at constant pressure. If γ = Cp/Cv then increase in internal energy of the gas is
questions.explanation: ΔQ = ΔU + ΔW ΔQ - ΔW = ΔU W/γ-1 = ΔU ΔW = nRΔT = nR(2T - T) = nRT = 2RT ΔU = 2RT/γ-1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A triatomic, diatomic and monatomic gas is supplied same amount of heat at constant pressure, then

options.A: Fractional energy used to change internal energy is maximum in monatomic gas
options.B: Fractional energy used to change internal energy is maximum in diatomic gas
options.C: Fractional energy used to change internal energy is maximum in triatomic gases
options.D: Fractional energy used to change internal energy is same in all the three gases

questions.explanation: ΔU/ΔQ = nCvΔT/nCpΔT = 1/γ γ_mono = 5/3, ΔU/ΔQ = 3/5 = 0.6 γ_dia = 7/5, ΔU/ΔQ = 5/7 ≈ 0.714 γ_tria = 4/3, ΔU/ΔQ = 3/4 = 0.75 Fractional energy used to change internal energy is maximum in Triatomic gas.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: 105 calories of heat is required to raise the temperature of 3 moles of an ideal gas at constant pressure from 30°C to 35°C. The amount of heat required in calories to raise the temperature of the gas through the range (60°C to 65°C) at constant volume is (γ = 1.4)
questions.explanation: At constant pressure heat absorbed = ΔQ = nCpΔT …(1) At constant volume heat absorbed = ΔU = nCvΔT …(2) Dividing (1) by (2), ΔQ/ΔU = Cp/Cv = γ = 1.4 105/ΔU = 1.4 ⇒ ΔU = 75 cal

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: To an ideal triatomic gas 800 cal heat energy is given at constant pressure. If vibrational mode is neglected, then energy used by gas in work done against surroundings is
questions.explanation: Heat at constant pressure ΔQ = nCpΔT Heat for doing work ΔW = nRΔT Then ΔW/ΔQ = R/Cp = 1-1/γ γ for triatomic (neglecting vibration) = 4/3 ΔW = 800 * (1 - 3/4) = 800 * 1/4 = 200 cal

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A closed cylindrical vessel contains N moles of an ideal diatomic gas at a temperature T. On supplying heat, temperature remains same, but n moles get dissociated into atoms. The heat supplied is
questions.explanation: Heat supplied = ΔU = Ufinal - Uinitial Initial internal energy = 5/2 NRT Final internal energy when 'n' moles get dissociated = 5/2 (N - n)RT + 3/2 (2n)RT Ufinal = 5/2 (N - n)RT + 3nRT ΔU = - 5/2 NRT = 5/2 NRT - 5/2 nRT + 3nRT - 5/2 NRT = nRT(3 - 5/2) = 1/2 nRT

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Figure shows the isotherms of a fixed mass of an ideal gas at three temperatures TA, TB and TC, then
questions.explanation: ∵ PV = RT P = RT/V For constant 1/V, So, P ∝ T ∵ PC > PB > PA then TC > TB > TA

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Slope of isotherm for a gas (having γ = 5/3) is 3 × 105 N/m2. If the same gas is undergoing adiabatic change then adiabatic elasticity at that instant is
questions.explanation: Adiabatic elasticity = γP = γ * (Isothermal Elasticity) P is the same. Slope of isotherm gives P? Actually, for an isothermal process, the bulk modulus is P. So P = 3 × 105 N/m2. Adiabatic elasticity = (5/3) * 3 × 105 = 5 × 105 N/m2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A gas may expand either adiabatically or isothermally. A number of P–V curves are drawn for the two processes over different range of pressure and volume. It will be found that

options.A: An adiabatic curve and an isothermal curve may intersect
options.B: Two adiabatic curves do not intersect
options.C: Two isothermal curves do not intersect

questions.explanation: Slope for isothermal and adiabatic are not same so they will intersect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Figure shows, the adiabatic curve on a log T and log V scale performed on ideal gas. The gas is
questions.explanation: TVγ-1 = K log T + (γ-1) log V = 0 log T = - (γ-1) log V y = - (γ - 1) x Slope from graph = (4-2)/(2-1) = 2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A diatomic gas undergoes a process represented by PV1.3 = constant. Choose the incorrect statement

options.A: The gas expands by absorbing heat from the surroundings
options.B: The gas cools down during expansion
options.C: The work done by surroundings during expansion of the gas is negative

questions.explanation: PV1.3 = K W = (P1V1 - P2V2)/(n-1) ∵ n > 1, so W is negative. Heat supplied by surrounding heat goes to do work. ∴ Down when expands.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The process CD is shown in the diagram. As system is taken from C to D, what happens to the temperature of the system?

options.A: Temperature first decreases and then increases
options.B: Temperature first increases and then decreases

questions.explanation: For a point on the curve, PV = constant for an isothermal. Here, at C: 3P0V0, at D: 3P0V0. So start and end are on the same isotherm. However, for points in between, PV > P0V0? Actually, for a curve that is less steep than an isothermal? This is a straight line from C to D. The product PV is maximum somewhere in the middle, hence temperature first increases then decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A P-T graph is shown for a cyclic process. Select correct statement regarding this

options.A: During process CD, work done by gas is negative
options.B: During process AB, work done by the gas is positive
options.C: During process BC internal energy of system increases
options.D: During process BC internal energy of the system decreases

questions.explanation: In process BC (isochoric process) where ΔT is (+)ive. So ΔU = nCvΔT ∵ ΔT is positive ⇒ U increases

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An ideal gas expands according to the law P2V = constant. The internal energy of the gas
questions.explanation: P2V = K or PV–2 = K? Actually P2V = K => P ∝ V-1/2. This is not a standard polytropic form PVn = K. Let's write it as P * V1/2 = K'? Not correct. Better: P2V = K => (nRT/V)2 * V = K => n2R2T2 / V = K => T2 ∝ V. So as V increases, T increases. Internal energy U ∝ T, so U increases continuously.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: 4.0 g of a gas occupies 22.4 litres at NTP. The specific heat capacity of the gas at constant volume is 5.0 JK–1 mol–1. If the speed of sound in this gas at NTP is 952 ms–1, then the heat capacity at constant pressure is (Take gas constant R = 8.3 JK–1 mol–1)
questions.explanation: Molecular weight M = mass/ no of moles? 4g at NTP occupies 22.4L, so it's 4g/mol? Actually, 22.4L at NTP is 1 mole, so 4g = 1 mole, so M = 4 g/mol. γ = Cp/Cv. Speed of sound v = √(γRT/M) 952 = √(γ * 8.3 * 273 / 0.004) γ = (9522 * 0.004) / (8.3 * 273) ≈ (906304 * 0.004) / 2265.9 ≈ 3625.2 / 2265.9 ≈ 1.6 Cp = γ * Cv = 1.6 * 5 = 8.0 J/K/mol.

questions.question: During an adiabatic process, the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its temperature. The ratio of Cp/Cv for the gas is:
questions.explanation: P ∝ T3 PT-3 = constant For an adiabatic process, P1-γ Tγ = constant. So, γ/(1-γ) = -3 γ = -3(1-γ) => γ = -3 + 3γ => -2γ = -3 => γ = 3/2

questions.explanation: ∵ PV = RT V = (R/P) T Slope of V-T graph = R/P θ2 > θ1 so tanθ2 > tanθ1 ⇒ R/P2 > R/P1 ⇒ 1/P2 > 1/P1 ⇒ P2 < P1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A mass of diatomic gas (γ = 1.4) at a pressure of 2 atmospheres is compressed adiabatically so that its temperature rises from 27°C to 927°C. The pressure of the gas in the final state is
questions.explanation: For adiabatic process, P1-γ Tγ = constant. P(1-γ)/γ ∝ T? Better use P1-γ Tγ = constant. (P2/P1)1-γ = (T1/T2)γ (P2/2)1-1.4 = (300/1200)1.4 (P2/2)-0.4 = (1/4)1.4 (P2/2)0.4 = 41.4 = 4(7/5) = (22)(7/5) = 214/5 = 22.8 Taking both sides to the power 5/2: P2/2 = (22.8)5/2 = 27 = 128 P2 = 256 atm

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: A: Every metal has a definite work function, still all photoelectrons do not come out with the same energy if incident radiation is monochromatic. R: Work function is the minimum energy required for the electron in the highest level of the conduction band to get out of the metal. Not all electrons in the metal belong to this level rather they occupy a continuous band of levels.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Sol. Answer (1)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: A: Work function of aluminium is 4.2eV. Emission of electrons will be possible by two photons, each of 2.5eV energy, striking the electron of aluminium. R: Energy of a photon can be less than the work function of the metal, for photoelectron emission.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Sol. Answer (4) Both the assertion and reason are incorrect. The quantised nature of light allows emission only when the photon striking the electron has enough energy to eject the electron. There will be no emission due to photons with energy less than work function.

R: Photoelectric current is independent of intensity but increases with increasing frequency of incident radiation.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Sol. Answer (4) On increasing the intensity of light only number of photoelectrons increase and not the KE of electrons or photons.

options.B: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Sol. Answer (1) The process of photoelectric emission depends of frequency of wave and thermionic emission occurs when metal is heated.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dimensional formula for the product of pressure and volume is the same as that for:
questions.explanation: Pressure = Force/Area = [ML⁻¹T⁻²]. Volume = [L³]. Therefore, Pressure × Volume = [ML⁻¹T⁻²] × [L³] = [ML²T⁻²]. This is the dimensional formula for work and energy. (As stated in NCERT, Appendix A9, entries for Work/Energy and Pressure energy).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the error in the measurement of the radius of a sphere is 2%, then the error in the determination of its volume will be:
questions.explanation: Volume V = (4/3)πR³. The relative error in volume, ΔV/V = 3(ΔR/R). Given ΔR/R = 2% = 0.02, so ΔV/V = 3 × 0.02 = 0.06 or 6%. (This follows from the rules for combination of errors discussed in NCERT Chapter 1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of 20 m/s from the top of a multistorey building of height 25 m. The time taken by the ball to hit the ground is (Take g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: Taking the point of projection as origin and upward direction as positive. Using the second equation of motion for displacement, s=ut+12at2s=ut+21at2, where s = -25 m (as final position is 25 m below the starting point), u = +20 m/s, and a = -g = -10 m/s². The equation becomes: −25=20t−5t2−25=20t−5t2. Rearranging gives 5t2−20t−25=05t2−20t−25=0 or t2−4t−5=0t2−4t−5=0. Solving, (t-5)(t+1)=0, so t = 5 s. (This is exactly the problem from NCERT Example 2.3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Rain is falling vertically with a speed of 35 m/s. Wind starts blowing after sometime with a speed of 12 m/s in the east to west direction. In which direction should a boy waiting at a bus stop hold his umbrella?

options.A: At an angle of tan⁻¹(12/35) with the vertical towards west
options.B: At an angle of tan⁻¹(35/12) with the vertical towards east
options.C: At an angle of tan⁻¹(12/35) with the vertical towards east
options.D: At an angle of 19° with the vertical towards west

questions.explanation: The boy should hold his umbrella in the direction of the relative velocity of the rain with respect to the ground. The velocity of rain (v⃗r=35vr=35 m/s downward) and the velocity of wind (v⃗w=12vw=12 m/s east to west) are perpendicular. The resultant velocity makes an angle θθ with the vertical, where tan⁡θ=vwvr=1235tanθ=vrvw=3512. The direction is towards west. (This is directly from NCERT Example 3.1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block of mass m is placed on a rough inclined plane of inclination θ. The coefficient of friction between the block and the plane is μ. The angle of repose is given by:
questions.explanation: The angle of repose is the maximum angle of inclination at which a block remains at rest on an incline without slipping. At this angle, the component of gravity down the plane (mg sin θ) equals the maximum static friction force (μ mg cos θ). Thus, tan θ = μ, or θ = tan⁻¹(μ). (This is derived from the equilibrium condition in NCERT Chapter 4, Example 4.8).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A constant retarding force of 50 N is applied to a body of mass 20 kg moving initially with a speed of 15 m/s. How long does the body take to stop?
questions.explanation: From Newton's second law, acceleration a = F/m = -50 N / 20 kg = -2.5 m/s² (negative because it's retardation). Using the first equation of motion, v = u + at, where v=0, u=15 m/s, and a = -2.5 m/s². 0 = 15 - 2.5t, which gives t = 15/2.5 = 6 s. (This is directly from NCERT Exercise 4.5).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A gun of mass 100 kg fires a shell of mass 0.02 kg. If the muzzle speed of the shell is 80 m/s, what is the recoil speed of the gun?
questions.explanation: By conservation of linear momentum, initial momentum (gun + shell) = 0. After firing, momentum of gun (Mv) + momentum of shell (m u) = 0. So Mv + m u = 0 => v = - (m u)/M. The negative sign indicates opposite direction. The magnitude is v = (0.02 kg * 80 m/s) / 100 kg = 1.6 / 100 = 0.016 m/s. (This is from NCERT Exercise 4.19).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bullet of mass 0.04 kg moving with a speed of 90 m/s enters a heavy wooden block and is stopped after a distance of 60 cm. What is the average resistive force exerted by the block on the bullet?
questions.explanation: Initial kinetic energy = (1/2)mv² = 0.5 * 0.04 * 8100 = 162 J. This energy is dissipated as work done by the resistive force, which is F * d. So F * 0.6 m = 162 J, hence F = 162 / 0.6 = 270 N. Alternatively, using v² - u² = 2as, retardation a = -u²/2s, and F = m|a|. (This is NCERT Example 4.2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block of mass m = 1 kg moving on a horizontal surface with speed vi=2vi=2 m/s enters a rough patch ranging from x=0.10x=0.10 m to x=2.01x=2.01 m. The retarding force FrFr on the block in this range is Fr=−k/xFr=−k/x for 0.1<x<2.010.1<x<2.01 m, and 0 elsewhere, where k=0.5k=0.5 J. The final kinetic energy of the block as it crosses this patch is:
questions.explanation: Using work-energy theorem, final KE = initial KE + work done by force. Work done by Fr=∫xixfFrdx=∫0.12.01(−k/x)dx=−k[ln⁡x]0.12.01=−0.5ln⁡(2.01/0.1)=−0.5ln⁡(20.1)≈−0.5×3=−1.5JFr=∫xixfFrdx=∫0.12.01(−k/x)dx=−k[lnx]0.12.01=−0.5ln(2.01/0.1)=−0.5ln(20.1)≈−0.5×3=−1.5J (since ln 20 ≈ 3). Initial KE = 1/2 * 1 * 2² = 2 J. Final KE = 2 + (-1.5) = 0.5 J. (This is NCERT Example 5.6).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bob of mass m is suspended by a light string of length L. It is imparted a horizontal velocity v0v0 at the lowest point such that it completes a semi-circular trajectory in the vertical plane. The ratio of its kinetic energies at points B (at the same horizontal level as the centre of the circle) and C (the topmost point) is:
questions.explanation: From NCERT Example 5.7, for the bob to just complete the circle, v0=5gLv0=5gL. The speed at the topmost point C is vc=gLvc=gL. The speed at point B (at the same level as the centre) is vB=3gLvB=3gL. Therefore, KB/KC=(12mvB2)/(12mvC2)=(3gL)/(gL)=3/1KB/KC=(21mvB2)/(21mvC2)=(3gL)/(gL)=3/1. So the ratio is 3:1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A spring of force constant k is cut into two pieces of lengths in the ratio 1:2. The force constant of the longer piece is:
questions.explanation: For a spring, force constant k ∝ 1/l (original length). If the original length is L, the pieces have lengths L/3 and 2L/3. The force constant for a piece is inversely proportional to its length. So for the piece of length L/3, k₁ = (L/(L/3)) k = 3k. For the piece of length 2L/3, k₂ = (L/(2L/3)) k = (3/2)k. The question asks for the longer piece, which is (3/2)k. (This is an application of the spring force concept from NCERT Chapter 5).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The centre of mass of three particles of masses 100 g, 150 g, and 200 g located at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side 0.5 m. If the 100 g mass is at the origin (0,0) and the 150 g mass is at (0.5, 0) m, the coordinates of the 200 g mass are (0.25, 0.25√3) m. The x-coordinate of the centre of mass is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A uniform rod of mass M and length L is suspended about its end. Its moment of inertia about this axis is I. If the rod is broken into two equal halves and one half is suspended about its end, its moment of inertia would be:
questions.explanation: For the original rod of mass M and length L, moment of inertia about one end, I=ML23I=3ML2. For a half-rod, mass = M/2, length = L/2. Its moment of inertia about its own end is I′=(M/2)(L/2)23=(M/2)(L2/4)3=ML224I′=3(M/2)(L/2)2=3(M/2)(L2/4)=24ML2. The ratio I′/I=ML2/24ML2/3=324=18I′/I=ML2/3ML2/24=243=81. So, I′=I/8I′=I/8. (Based on the formula for moment of inertia of a rod from NCERT Table 6.1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A solid sphere of mass M and radius R is rolling without slipping on a horizontal surface. Its total kinetic energy is:
questions.explanation: For a rolling object, total KE = translational KE + rotational KE = (1/2)Mv² + (1/2)Iω². For a solid sphere, I = (2/5)MR² and ω = v/R. So rotational KE = (1/2)*(2/5)MR²*(v²/R²) = (1/5)Mv². Total KE = (1/2 + 1/5)Mv² = (7/10)Mv². (This is a standard result derived from concepts in NCERT Chapter 6).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The gravitational force between two identical objects at a separation of 1 m is 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N. The mass of each object is:
questions.explanation: Gravitational force F = G m₁m₂/r². Given F = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N, r = 1 m, and m₁ = m₂ = m. So, 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹) * m * m / 1². Cancelling 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ from both sides gives m² = 1, so m = 1 kg. (This is a direct application of the Universal Law of Gravitation from NCERT Chapter 7).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The acceleration due to gravity at a height h above the surface of the earth is the same as that at a depth d below the surface. Then:
questions.explanation: Acceleration due to gravity at height h is gh=g(1−2h/Re)gh=g(1−2h/Re) for h << R_e. At depth d, gd=g(1−d/Re)gd=g(1−d/Re). Equating them: g(1−2h/Re)=g(1−d/Re)g(1−2h/Re)=g(1−d/Re) => -2h/R_e = -d/R_e => d = 2h. (This is derived from the formulas in NCERT Chapter 7, Section 7.6).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A satellite is in a circular orbit of radius R around the earth. The minimum energy required to shift it to a circular orbit of radius 2R is (where M is mass of earth, m is mass of satellite):
questions.explanation: Total energy of a satellite in an orbit of radius r is E=−GMm2rE=−2rGMm. Energy in orbit of radius R: E1=−GMm2RE1=−2RGMm. Energy in orbit of radius 2R: E2=−GMm4RE2=−4RGMm. The energy required to shift is the change in energy, ΔE=E2−E1=−GMm4R−(−GMm2R)=−GMm4R+GMm2R=GMm4RΔE=E2−E1=−4RGMm−(−2RGMm)=−4RGMm+2RGMm=4RGMm. This is positive, meaning energy must be supplied. (This is derived from the formula for energy of an orbiting satellite in NCERT Chapter 7, Section 7.10).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire of length L and cross-sectional area A is made of a material of Young's modulus Y. If the wire is stretched by an amount x, the work done is:
questions.explanation: Work done in stretching a wire is stored as elastic potential energy. This energy is given by U=12×stress×strain×volume=12×(YAxL)×(xL)×(AL)=12×YAxL×xL×ALU=21×stress×strain×volume=21×(LYAx)×(Lx)×(AL)=21×LYAx×Lx×AL. Wait, that's not simplifying correctly. Actually, stress = Y * strain = Y (x/L). Force at extension x is not constant. Work done = ∫0xFdx=∫0xYALxdx=YALx22∫0xFdx=∫0xLYAxdx=LYA2x2. This is the correct expression. (This is derived from the concept of elastic potential energy in a stretched wire, NCERT Chapter 8, Section 8.5.5).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A steel wire and a copper wire of the same length and same cross-sectional area are stretched by the same weight. If the Young's moduli of steel and copper are Y_s and Y_c respectively, with Y_s > Y_c, then:

options.A: Both wires will have the same elongation.
options.D: The elongation depends on the weight, not the material.

questions.explanation: Elongation ΔL=FLAYΔL=AYFL. Since F (weight), L, and A are the same for both wires, elongation is inversely proportional to Young's modulus. As Y_s > Y_c, the copper wire, with a smaller Young's modulus, will elongate more. (This is a direct application of Young's modulus formula from NCERT Chapter 8).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The pressure at a point in a liquid at rest depends on the:

options.A: Depth of the point below the surface only.
options.B: Depth and density of the liquid only.
options.C: Depth, density of the liquid, and acceleration due to gravity.
options.D: Depth and the shape of the container.

questions.explanation: The pressure at a depth h in a liquid of density ρ is given by P = Pₐ + ρgh, where g is the acceleration due to gravity. Thus, it depends on depth, density, and g. It is independent of the shape of the container. (From NCERT Chapter 9, Section 9.2.2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capillary tube of radius r is immersed in a liquid of density ρ and surface tension S. The angle of contact is zero. The height h to which the liquid rises in the tube is given by:
questions.explanation: The capillary rise formula is h=2Scos⁡θρgrh=ρgr2Scosθ. For an angle of contact of 0°, cos θ = 1, so h=2Sρgrh=ρgr2S. (This is from NCERT Chapter 9, Section 9.6.5).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a thermodynamic process, 500 J of heat is supplied to a gas and 200 J of work is done by the gas. The change in internal energy of the gas is:
questions.explanation: According to the first law of thermodynamics, ΔU = ΔQ - ΔW, where ΔQ is heat supplied to the system and ΔW is work done by the system. Here, ΔQ = +500 J and ΔW = +200 J. So, ΔU = 500 J - 200 J = 300 J. (This is a direct application of the first law from NCERT Chapter 11).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion with an amplitude A and time period T. The time taken by the particle to go from x = A/2 to x = A is:
questions.explanation: For SHM, x = A sin(ωt) if starting from mean position. For x = A/2, sin(ωt₁) = 1/2 => ωt₁ = π/6 => t₁ = π/(6ω). For x = A, sin(ωt₂) = 1 => ωt₂ = π/2 => t₂ = π/(2ω). Time taken = t₂ - t₁ = π/(2ω) - π/(6ω) = π/(3ω) = (π/3)/(2π/T) = T/6. (This is a standard SHM problem based on concepts from NCERT Chapter 13).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A simple pendulum of length L has a time period T. If the length is increased by 2%, the percentage change in its time period is approximately:
questions.explanation: Time period T = 2π√(L/g). Taking logs and differentiating, ΔT/T = (1/2)(ΔL/L). Given ΔL/L = 2% = 0.02, so ΔT/T = (1/2)*0.02 = 0.01 or 1%. (This is an application of the simple pendulum formula from NCERT Chapter 13).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wave travelling in the positive x-direction is given by y = A sin(ωt - kx). The wave velocity and the maximum particle velocity are related as v_wave = λ * ν. The ratio of the maximum particle velocity to the wave velocity is:
questions.explanation: Wave velocity, v_w = ω/k. Particle velocity, v_p = dy/dt = -Aω cos(ωt - kx). Maximum particle velocity, v_p(max) = Aω. The ratio v_p(max) / v_w = Aω / (ω/k) = A k. So the ratio is kA. (This is from NCERT Chapter 14).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A string is stretched between two fixed points and is vibrating in its third harmonic. The number of nodes and antinodes in the string are respectively:
questions.explanation: For a string fixed at both ends, the number of antinodes equals the harmonic number. For the third harmonic, there are 3 antinodes. The number of nodes is always one more than the number of antinodes, so there are 4 nodes (including the two fixed ends). (This is from the discussion on standing waves in NCERT Chapter 14).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency 5 MHz travels in vacuum along the positive z-direction. The electric field is along the x-direction. The amplitude of the electric field is 1.2 V/m. The amplitude of the magnetic field is:
questions.explanation: In an electromagnetic wave, the amplitudes of electric and magnetic fields are related by E₀ = c B₀, where c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s. So B₀ = E₀/c = 1.2 / (3 × 10⁸) = 4 × 10⁻⁹ T. (This is from NCERT Chapter 8).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following statements is NOT true about a plane electromagnetic wave propagating in free space?

options.A: The energy is equally divided between the electric and magnetic fields.
options.B: The electric and magnetic fields are in phase.
options.C: The wave carries momentum as well as energy.
options.D: The electric and magnetic fields are perpendicular to each other and parallel to the direction of propagation.

questions.explanation: In an electromagnetic wave, the electric and magnetic fields are perpendicular to each other and both are perpendicular to the direction of propagation. Option d states they are parallel to the direction of propagation, which is false. All other statements are true. (This is from NCERT Chapter 8).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two point charges +q and -q are placed at a distance 2a apart. The electric field at a point on the equatorial plane at a distance r from the centre of the dipole is proportional to:
questions.explanation: The electric field due to a dipole on its equatorial plane is E=−14πϵ0pr3E=−4πϵ01r3p for r >> a, where p = q(2a) is the dipole moment. Thus, E ∝ 1/r³. (From NCERT Chapter 1, Section 1.10.1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates has a capacitance C. If the distance between the plates is halved and the space between them is filled with a dielectric of dielectric constant 6, the new capacitance will be:
questions.explanation: Initial capacitance C = ε₀A/d. New distance d' = d/2, new dielectric constant K = 6. New capacitance C' = K ε₀A / d' = 6 ε₀A / (d/2) = 12 ε₀A/d = 12C. (This is NCERT Exercise 2.5).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A current of 1 A is flowing through a conductor. The number of electrons flowing per second through any cross-section of the conductor is approximately:
questions.explanation: Current I = ne/t, where n is the number of electrons flowing per second. So n = I / e = 1 A / (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C) = 6.25 × 10¹⁸ electrons/s. (This is a standard calculation from NCERT Chapter 3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The resistance of a wire is 5 Ω at 50°C and 6 Ω at 100°C. The resistance of the wire at 0°C will be:
questions.explanation: The variation of resistance with temperature is given by R_T = R₀(1 + αT). So, 5 = R₀(1 + 50α) and 6 = R₀(1 + 100α). Dividing the second by the first: 6/5 = (1+100α)/(1+50α) => 1.2(1+50α) = 1+100α => 1.2 + 60α = 1 + 100α => 0.2 = 40α => α = 0.005. Then, 5 = R₀(1 + 50*0.005) = R₀(1 + 0.25) = 1.25 R₀ => R₀ = 5/1.25 = 4 Ω. (This is based on NCERT Chapter 3, Section 3.8).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire of length L and resistance R is stretched so that its length becomes twice. Its new resistance will be:
questions.explanation: When a wire is stretched, its volume (V = A L) remains constant. If length becomes 2L, then area becomes A/2. Resistance R = ρ L/A. New resistance R' = ρ (2L) / (A/2) = ρ (2L) * (2/A) = 4 ρ L/A = 4R. (This is based on the formula R = ρL/A from NCERT Chapter 3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A charged particle of mass m and charge q enters a uniform magnetic field B with a velocity v perpendicular to the field. The time period of its circular motion is:
questions.explanation: The magnetic force provides the centripetal force: qvB = mv²/r => r = mv/qB. The time period T = 2πr/v = 2π(mv/qB)/v = 2πm/qB. (This is derived in NCERT Chapter 4, Section 4.3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of radius R carrying current I is B. If the current through the coil is doubled and its radius is halved, the magnetic field at its centre will become:
questions.explanation: Magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil is B = μ₀ I / (2R). New current I' = 2I, new radius R' = R/2. New field B' = μ₀ (2I) / (2 * (R/2)) = μ₀ (2I) / R = 4 * (μ₀ I / (2R)) = 4B. (This is from the Biot-Savart law application in NCERT Chapter 4).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A rectangular coil of area A, number of turns N, is placed in a uniform magnetic field B. The coil is free to rotate. The torque on the coil is maximum when the angle between the magnetic moment and the field is:
questions.explanation: Torque τ = m B sin θ, where m = NIA is the magnetic moment. The torque is maximum when sin θ is maximum, i.e., sin θ = 1, which gives θ = 90°. (From NCERT Chapter 4, Section 4.9.1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Which of the following physical quantities has the same dimensional formula as energy?
questions.explanation: Energy has dimensions [ML²T⁻²]. Torque = Force × distance = [MLT⁻²] × [L] = [ML²T⁻²]. (NCERT, Chapter 1 & 6).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The velocity-time graph of a body moving in a straight line is a straight line parallel to the time axis. The body is in:
questions.explanation: When the velocity-time graph is parallel to the time axis, velocity remains constant. Hence the body is in uniform velocity. (NCERT, Chapter 2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A car starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at 2 m/s² for 10 seconds. The distance covered by the car in this time interval is:
questions.explanation: Using s = ut + ½at², with u = 0, a = 2 m/s², t = 10 s, we get s = 0 + ½ × 2 × 100 = 100 m. (NCERT, Chapter 2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of 20 m/s. The maximum height reached is (take g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: Maximum height H = u²/2g = (20)²/(2 × 10) = 400/20 = 20 m. (NCERT, Chapter 2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two vectors A and B have magnitudes 3 units and 4 units respectively. |A + B| = 5 units. The angle between A and B is:
questions.explanation: R² = A² + B² + 2AB cosθ. 25 = 9 + 16 + 2×3×4 cosθ = 25 + 24 cosθ. Therefore, 24 cosθ = 0 ⇒ cosθ = 0 ⇒ θ = 90°. (NCERT, Chapter 3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A projectile is thrown with velocity u at an angle θ with the horizontal. Its time of flight is given by:
questions.explanation: Time of flight T = 2u sinθ/g. (NCERT, Chapter 3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body of mass 5 kg is acted upon by a constant force of 20 N. The acceleration produced is:
questions.explanation: F = ma ⇒ a = F/m = 20/5 = 4 m/s². (NCERT, Chapter 4).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Newton's first law of motion gives the concept of:
questions.explanation: Newton's first law is also called the law of inertia. (NCERT, Chapter 4).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 5 kg rifle fires a 10 g bullet with a speed of 500 m/s. The recoil speed of the rifle is:
questions.explanation: By conservation of momentum: m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = 0. 5 × V + 0.01 × 500 = 0 ⇒ 5V = -5 ⇒ V = -1 m/s. Magnitude = 1 m/s. (NCERT, Chapter 4).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A force F = (2i + 3j) N acts on a particle and displaces it by d = (4i - j) m. The work done by the force is:
questions.explanation: Work done W = F·d = (2)(4) + (3)(-1) = 8 - 3 = 5 J. (NCERT, Chapter 5).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The kinetic energy of a body of mass 2 kg moving with velocity 3 m/s is:
questions.explanation: KE = ½ mv² = ½ × 2 × 9 = 9 J. (NCERT, Chapter 5).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A spring of force constant k is stretched by a distance x. The potential energy stored in the spring is:
questions.explanation: Elastic potential energy of a spring is U = ½ kx². (NCERT, Chapter 5).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The centre of mass of a system of particles depends on:

options.C: Both masses and positions of particles

questions.explanation: Centre of mass R = (∑mᵢrᵢ)/∑mᵢ. It depends on both masses and their positions. (NCERT, Chapter 6).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A solid sphere of radius R has its moment of inertia about its diameter as I. The moment of inertia about a tangent parallel to the diameter is:
questions.explanation: I_diameter = (2/5)MR². Using parallel axis theorem, I_tangent = I_diameter + MR² = (2/5 + 1)MR² = (7/5)MR² = (7/5)I. (NCERT, Chapter 6).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Kepler's second law (law of areas) is a consequence of:
questions.explanation: Law of areas follows directly from the fact that angular momentum is conserved for a central force like gravity. (NCERT, Chapter 7).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The acceleration due to gravity at a height R (R = radius of earth) above the earth's surface is (g at surface = 9.8 m/s²):
questions.explanation: g' = g[R/(R+h)]². At h = R, g' = g[R/(2R)]² = g/4 = 9.8/4 = 2.45 m/s². (NCERT, Chapter 7).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A satellite orbits close to the earth's surface. Its orbital velocity is approximately:
questions.explanation: Orbital velocity near earth's surface v₀ = √(gR) ≈ √(9.8 × 6.4×10⁶) ≈ 7.92 km/s ≈ 8 km/s. (NCERT, Chapter 7).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Stress is defined as:
questions.explanation: Stress = Restoring force per unit area. (NCERT, Chapter 8).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire of length 2 m and cross-sectional area 10⁻⁶ m² has a Young's modulus 2 × 10¹¹ N/m². The elongation produced when a load of 4 kg is suspended is (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: Stress = F/A = 40/10⁻⁶ = 4×10⁷ N/m². Strain = stress/Y = 4×10⁷/2×10¹¹ = 2×10⁻⁴. Elongation = strain × L = 2×10⁻⁴ × 2 = 4×10⁻⁴ m = 0.4 mm. (NCERT, Chapter 8).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A liquid is flowing through a pipe of non-uniform cross-section. At a point where the area of cross-section is 0.02 m², the velocity of liquid is 1 m/s. The velocity at another point where area is 0.01 m² is:
questions.explanation: By equation of continuity, A₁v₁ = A₂v₂. 0.02 × 1 = 0.01 × v₂ ⇒ v₂ = 2 m/s. (NCERT, Chapter 9).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Bernoulli's theorem is based on the conservation of:
questions.explanation: Bernoulli's theorem is essentially the conservation of energy per unit volume for an ideal fluid. (NCERT, Chapter 9).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Water rises to a height h in a capillary tube. If the radius of the tube is halved, the height of water rise will become:
questions.explanation: Capillary rise h ∝ 1/r. If radius is halved, height becomes twice. (NCERT, Chapter 9).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following has the highest specific heat capacity?
questions.explanation: Water has the highest specific heat capacity (4186 J kg⁻¹ K⁻¹) among common substances. (NCERT, Chapter 10).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A copper rod of length 1 m is heated from 0°C to 100°C. If coefficient of linear expansion of copper is 1.7 × 10⁻⁵ °C⁻¹, the increase in length is:
questions.explanation: ΔL = αLΔT = 1.7×10⁻⁵ × 1 × 100 = 1.7×10⁻³ m = 1.7 mm. (NCERT, Chapter 10).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The first law of thermodynamics is a statement of conservation of:
questions.explanation: First law of thermodynamics (ΔQ = ΔU + ΔW) is the law of conservation of energy. (NCERT, Chapter 11).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas:
questions.explanation: For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature. In isothermal process, temperature constant ⇒ ΔU = 0. (NCERT, Chapter 11).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An ideal gas heat engine operates between 127°C and 27°C. Its efficiency is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The rms speed of oxygen molecules at 0°C is 460 m/s. The rms speed at 273°C will be:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The mean free path of gas molecules is λ. If the number density is doubled, the new mean free path becomes:
questions.explanation: Mean free path λ = 1/(√2 n πd²). λ ∝ 1/n. If n is doubled, λ becomes half. (NCERT, Chapter 12).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In simple harmonic motion, the acceleration of the particle is zero when:
questions.explanation: In SHM, a = -ω²x. a = 0 when x = 0 (mean position). At mean position, velocity is maximum. (NCERT, Chapter 13).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle executes SHM with amplitude A and time period T. Its maximum speed is:
questions.explanation: v_max = Aω = A × (2π/T) = 2πA/T. (NCERT, Chapter 13).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A simple pendulum of length 1 m has a time period of 2 s. The time period of another simple pendulum of length 4 m at the same place will be:
questions.explanation: T ∝ √L. T₂/T₁ = √(L₂/L₁) = √(4/1) = 2. So T₂ = 2 × 2 = 4 s. (NCERT, Chapter 13).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a transverse wave, the particles of the medium oscillate:

options.A: Along the direction of wave propagation
options.B: Perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation

questions.explanation: In transverse waves, particle motion is perpendicular to wave propagation. (NCERT, Chapter 14).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sound wave has a frequency of 500 Hz and wavelength 0.68 m. The speed of sound is:
questions.explanation: v = νλ = 500 × 0.68 = 340 m/s. (NCERT, Chapter 14).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a stationary wave, the distance between a node and the next antinode is:
questions.explanation: Distance between successive nodes or antinodes is λ/2. Distance between a node and next antinode is λ/4. (NCERT, Chapter 14).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two wires of the same material have lengths in the ratio 1:2 and radii in the ratio 2:1. The ratio of their resistances is:
questions.explanation: R = ρL/A = ρL/πr². R₁/R₂ = (L₁/L₂) × (r₂/r₁)² = (1/2) × (1/2)² = 1/2 × 1/4 = 1/8. (NCERT, Chapter 3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 4 Ω resistance wire is bent in the middle to form a V-shape. The resistance between the two ends is:
questions.explanation: Bending at middle divides the wire into two equal halves, each of resistance 2 Ω. These two are connected in parallel. Equivalent resistance = (2×2)/(2+2) = 1 Ω. (NCERT, Chapter 3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The potential difference across a 2 Ω resistor is 4 V. The current flowing through it is:
questions.explanation: Ohm's law: I = V/R = 4/2 = 2 A. (NCERT, Chapter 3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three capacitors of capacitances 2 μF, 3 μF, and 6 μF are connected in parallel. The equivalent capacitance is:
questions.explanation: In parallel, C = C₁ + C₂ + C₃ = 2 + 3 + 6 = 11 μF. (NCERT, Chapter 2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electron has a charge of 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. The number of electrons constituting 1 C of charge is:
questions.explanation: n = Q/e = 1 / (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 6.25 × 10¹⁸. (NCERT, Chapter 1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A charge of 2 μC is placed at a distance of 0.1 m from another charge of 5 μC in air. The force between them is (1/4πε₀ = 9 × 10⁹ N m²/C²):

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An infinitely long straight conductor carries a current of 5 A. The magnetic field at a distance of 10 cm from it is (μ₀/4π = 10⁻⁷ T m/A):

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A circular coil of 100 turns has radius 0.05 m and carries current 1 A. The magnetic field at its centre is (μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ T m/A):

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A straight wire of length 0.5 m carrying current 2 A is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of 0.1 T. The force on the wire is:
questions.explanation: F = BIL sinθ, with θ = 90°. F = 0.1 × 2 × 0.5 = 0.1 N. (NCERT, Chapter 4).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic flux linked with a coil changes from 2 Wb to 10 Wb in 0.2 s. The induced emf is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle moves along the x-axis such that its position x (in meters) varies with time t (in seconds) as x = t³ - 6t² + 9t + 2. The distance covered by the particle in the first 4 seconds is:
questions.explanation: v = dx/dt = 3t² - 12t + 9 = 3(t² - 4t + 3) = 3(t - 1)(t - 3). v = 0 at t = 1 s and t = 3 s. Positions: at t = 0, x = 2; t = 1, x = 1 - 6 + 9 + 2 = 6; t = 3, x = 27 - 54 + 27 + 2 = 2; t = 4, x = 64 - 96 + 36 + 2 = 6. Motion: 0→1 s: +4 m, 1→3 s: -4 m, 3→4 s: +4 m. Total distance = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12 m? Wait, check: 0→1: from 2 to 6 = +4. 1→3: from 6 to 2 = -4 (distance 4). 3→4: from 2 to 6 = +4. Total = 4+4+4 = 12 m. Option b is 12. Option c is 14. Did I miss? Let's recalc positions carefully: t=0: x=2. t=1: 1-6+9+2=6. t=3: 27-54+27+2=2. t=4: 64-96+36+2=6. Yes, distances: 0-1: |6-2|=4; 1-3: |2-6|=4; 3-4: |6-2|=4; total=12. So correct is 12 m, option b. (Combines kinematics, differentiation, and careful interpretation of distance vs displacement from NCERT Chapter 2 & 3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball is projected vertically upwards from the top of a tower of height 50 m with a velocity of 20 m/s. Another ball is simultaneously dropped from the same point. The time at which the distance between them becomes 30 m for the first time is (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: Positions from top: y₁ = 20t - 5t² (upward throw), y₂ = -5t² (dropped). Separation S = y₁ - y₂ = 20t. For S = 30 m, 20t = 30 ⇒ t = 1.5 s. This works while both are in motion. (NCERT Chapter 2, relative motion concept).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A projectile is projected from ground with speed u at an angle θ with horizontal. The radius of curvature of its trajectory at the point of projection is:
questions.explanation: At projection point, velocity = u, acceleration = g downward. Normal acceleration a_n = v²/R = u²/R. Also a_n = g cosθ (component of g perpendicular to velocity). Equating: u²/R = g cosθ ⇒ R = u²/(g cosθ). (NCERT Chapter 3 & 4, circular motion concept applied to projectile).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body of mass m is attached to a string of length L and whirled in a vertical circle. The difference in tension at the lowest point and highest point is:
questions.explanation: At lowest point: T₁ - mg = mv₁²/L. At highest point: T₂ + mg = mv₂²/L. By energy conservation: ½mv₁² = ½mv₂² + mg(2L) ⇒ v₁² - v₂² = 4gL. Subtracting the two equations: (T₁ - mg) - (T₂ + mg) = (mv₁²/L) - (mv₂²/L) ⇒ T₁ - T₂ - 2mg = m(v₁² - v₂²)/L = m(4gL)/L = 4mg ⇒ T₁ - T₂ = 6mg. (NCERT Chapter 5 & 4, circular motion with energy).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle of mass m is projected with velocity u at an angle θ with horizontal. The magnitude of its angular momentum about the point of projection when it is at the highest point is:
questions.explanation: At highest point, velocity = u cosθ horizontally. Height H = u² sin²θ/2g. Angular momentum L = m × (horizontal velocity) × (vertical distance from projection point) = m × (u cosθ) × H = m u cosθ × (u² sin²θ/2g) = (m u³ sin²θ cosθ)/(2g). (NCERT Chapter 3 & 6, projectile + angular momentum).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A solid sphere and a hollow sphere of the same mass and same radius are released simultaneously from the top of an inclined plane. They roll without slipping. Which one reaches the bottom first?

options.D: Depends on the angle of incline

questions.explanation: Acceleration a = g sinθ / (1 + I/mR²). For solid sphere I = 2/5 mR², so a_s = g sinθ / (1 + 2/5) = (5/7)g sinθ. For hollow sphere I = 2/3 mR², so a_h = g sinθ / (1 + 2/3) = (3/5)g sinθ. Since a_s > a_h, solid sphere reaches first. (NCERT Chapter 6, rotational motion).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A ring of mass M and radius R is rolling without slipping on a horizontal surface with speed v. The total kinetic energy of the ring is:
questions.explanation: For a ring, I = MR². Rotational KE = ½Iω² = ½(MR²)(v/R)² = ½Mv². Translational KE = ½Mv². Total = Mv². (NCERT Chapter 6).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A disc of mass M and radius R is rolling without slipping on a horizontal surface with angular velocity ω. The angular momentum of the disc about a point on the ground vertically below the centre is:
questions.explanation: Angular momentum about point on ground = L_cm + M(R × v_cm). Here L_cm = I_cm ω = (½MR²)ω, and M(R × v_cm) = M × R × (Rω) = MR²ω (direction same as L_cm). Total = (³⁄₂)MR²ω. (NCERT Chapter 6).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle is placed at a distance R/2 from the centre of a uniform spherical shell of mass M and radius R. The gravitational force on the particle is:
questions.explanation: Inside a uniform spherical shell, the gravitational field is zero everywhere. Hence force = 0. (NCERT Chapter 7).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two satellites of masses m and 2m are orbiting the earth in circular orbits of radii r and 2r respectively. The ratio of their orbital periods T₁/T₂ is:
questions.explanation: T² ∝ r³ (Kepler's law). T₁/T₂ = (r₁/r₂)⁽³/²⁾ ⁼ ⁽ʳ/²ʳ⁾(3/2) = (½)^(3/2) = 1/(2√2). Mass does not affect orbital period. (NCERT Chapter 7).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire of length L and cross-sectional area A is stretched by a force F. The elongation produced is x. If the same wire is stretched by a force 2F, the elongation will be:
questions.explanation: Within elastic limit, stress ∝ strain ⇒ F/A ∝ x/L ⇒ F ∝ x. So if force is doubled, elongation doubles. (NCERT Chapter 8).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A gas is compressed adiabatically from volume V₁ to V₂. The work done on the gas is given by:
questions.explanation: For adiabatic process, work done by gas = (P₁V₁ - P₂V₂)/(γ - 1). Work done on gas = - work done by gas = (P₂V₂ - P₁V₁)/(γ - 1). (NCERT Chapter 11).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The rms speed of oxygen molecules at temperature T is v. If the temperature is doubled and oxygen molecules dissociate into oxygen atoms, the new rms speed is:
questions.explanation: v_rms = √(3RT/M). For O₂, M = 32. For O atoms, M' = 16. T' = 2T. v'_rms = √(3R×2T/16) = √(6RT/16) = √(3RT/8). Original v = √(3RT/32). Ratio = √[(3RT/8)/(3RT/32)] = √(32/8) = √4 = 2. So new v_rms = 2v. (NCERT Chapter 12).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle executes SHM with amplitude A. At what displacement from mean position does its kinetic energy equal its potential energy?
questions.explanation: KE = ½mω²(A² - x²), PE = ½mω²x². Equating: A² - x² = x² ⇒ 2x² = A² ⇒ x = A/√2. (NCERT Chapter 13).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A simple pendulum of length L has time period T. If its length is increased by 44%, the percentage increase in its time period is:
questions.explanation: T ∝ √L. L' = 1.44L ⇒ T' = √1.44 T = 1.2T. Increase = 20%. (NCERT Chapter 13).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wave travelling along a string is given by y = 0.05 sin(10πt - 2πx) where x, y are in meters and t in seconds. The velocity of the wave is:
questions.explanation: Comparing with y = A sin(ωt - kx), ω = 10π rad/s, k = 2π rad/m. Wave velocity v = ω/k = 10π/2π = 5 m/s. (NCERT Chapter 14).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A point charge Q is placed at the centre of a cube of side L. The electric flux through one of its faces is:
questions.explanation: By Gauss's law, total flux through cube = Q/ε₀. By symmetry, flux through each of the 6 faces is equal. So flux through one face = Q/6ε₀. (NCERT Chapter 1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two capacitors of capacitances 2 μF and 4 μF are connected in series across a 12 V battery. The charge on each capacitor is:
questions.explanation: Series combination: 1/C = 1/2 + 1/4 = 3/4 ⇒ C = 4/3 μF. Charge Q = CV = (4/3) × 12 = 16 μC. In series, charge on each capacitor is same = 16 μC. (NCERT Chapter 2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 10 μF capacitor is charged to 100 V. The energy stored in it is:
questions.explanation: Energy = ½CV² = ½ × 10×10⁻⁶ × (100)² = ½ × 10⁻⁵ × 10⁴ = ½ × 10⁻¹ = 0.05 J. (NCERT Chapter 2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A current of 2 A flows through a wire for 1 minute. The charge that has passed through any cross-section is:
questions.explanation: Q = I × t = 2 × 60 = 120 C. (NCERT Chapter 3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire of resistance 10 Ω is stretched to twice its original length. The new resistance is:
questions.explanation: Volume constant: A₁L₁ = A₂L₂. If L₂ = 2L₁, then A₂ = A₁/2. R = ρL/A. R₂/R₁ = (L₂/L₁) × (A₁/A₂) = 2 × 2 = 4. So R₂ = 4 × 10 = 40 Ω. (NCERT Chapter 3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A galvanometer of resistance 50 Ω gives full-scale deflection for a current of 5 mA. To convert it into an ammeter of range 5 A, the required shunt resistance is approximately:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A proton and an α-particle enter a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly with the same speed. The ratio of the radii of their circular paths (r_p : r_α) is:
questions.explanation: r = mv/qB. For proton: m_p, q = e. For α-particle: m_α = 4m_p, q = 2e. r_p/r_α = (m_p/e) / (4m_p/2e) = (1) / (2) = 1:2. (NCERT Chapter 4).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A circular coil of 100 turns, radius 5 cm, carries a current of 0.5 A. The magnetic moment of the coil is:
questions.explanation: Magnetic moment M = NIA = 100 × 0.5 × π(0.05)² = 50 × π × 0.0025 = 50 × 0.00785 = 0.3925 Am² ≈ 0.393 Am². (NCERT Chapter 4).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic flux through a coil varies as Φ = 5t² + 3t + 2 weber. The induced emf at t = 2 s is:
questions.explanation: e = -dΦ/dt = -(10t + 3). At t = 2 s, e = -(20 + 3) = -23 V. Magnitude = 23 V. (NCERT Chapter 6).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A transformer has 500 turns in primary and 50 turns in secondary. If the primary voltage is 220 V, the secondary voltage is:
questions.explanation: V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p ⇒ V_s/220 = 50/500 = 1/10 ⇒ V_s = 22 V. (NCERT Chapter 7).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an AC circuit, the current lags behind the voltage by 90°. The circuit element is:
questions.explanation: In a purely inductive circuit, current lags voltage by 90°. (NCERT Chapter 7).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The displacement current between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor is 2 mA. The conduction current in the connecting wire is:
questions.explanation: Displacement current equals conduction current in the circuit. (NCERT Chapter 8).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electromagnetic wave has electric field E = E₀ sin(kz - ωt) î. The corresponding magnetic field is:
questions.explanation: For wave propagating in +z direction with E along x, B is along y (perpendicular) and in phase. B₀ = E₀/c. (NCERT Chapter 8).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The work function of a metal is 4 eV. The threshold wavelength for photoelectric emission is approximately:
questions.explanation: φ = hc/λ₀ ⇒ λ₀ = hc/φ = (1240 eV·nm)/4 eV = 310 nm. (NCERT Chapter 11).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential difference of 100 V is approximately:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In Rutherford's α-particle scattering experiment, the number of α-particles scattered at 60° is 100. The number scattered at 90° will be approximately:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The radius of the innermost orbit of hydrogen atom is 0.53 Å. The radius of the third orbit is:
questions.explanation: rₙ = n² r₁. For n = 3, r₃ = 9 × 0.53 Å = 4.77 Å. (NCERT Chapter 12).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The energy of the first excited state of hydrogen atom is:
questions.explanation: Ground state (n=1): -13.6 eV. First excited state (n=2): -13.6/4 = -3.4 eV. (NCERT Chapter 12).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The half-life of a radioactive substance is 10 days. The time taken for 75% of the sample to decay is:
questions.explanation: 75% decay means 25% remains. After n half-lives, fraction remaining = (½)ⁿ. (½)ⁿ = ¼ ⇒ n = 2. Time = 2 × 10 = 20 days. (NCERT Chapter 13).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The binding energy per nucleon is maximum for nuclei with mass number around:
questions.explanation: Binding energy per nucleon curve peaks around A = 56 (iron). (NCERT Chapter 13).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a p-n junction diode, the reverse saturation current is due to flow of:
questions.explanation: Reverse saturation current is due to minority carriers drifting across the junction. (NCERT Chapter 14).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A full-wave rectifier uses two diodes. If the input frequency is 50 Hz, the output frequency is:
questions.explanation: In full-wave rectifier, output frequency is twice the input frequency = 100 Hz. (NCERT Chapter 14).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The Boolean expression for the output Y of a two-input NAND gate is:
questions.explanation: NAND gate output Y = (A·B)̅ = A̅ + B̅ (by De Morgan's theorem). (NCERT Chapter 14).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The velocity of a projectile at the initial point A is (2î + 3ĵ) m/s. Its velocity (in m/s) at point B is:
questions.explanation: In projectile motion, the x-component of velocity remains unchanged (horizontal velocity is constant). The y-component reverses at the same horizontal level. So vx = 2 m/s (unchanged), vy = −3 m/s (reversed). Hence velocity at B = 2î − 3ĵ m/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two particles A and B are connected by a rigid rod AB. The rod slides along perpendicular rails as shown. The velocity of A to the right is 10 m/s. What is the velocity of B when angle α = 60°?
questions.explanation: From the constraint: y = x tan α. Differentiating: vy = vx tan α. Here vx = 10 m/s and α = 60°. So vy = 10 × tan 60° = 10√3 = 17.3 m/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following graphs represents the variation of resistivity (ρ) with temperature (T) for copper?
questions.explanation: For metals like copper, resistivity increases with temperature but not linearly — it increases at a decreasing rate at lower temperatures and more linearly at higher temperatures, giving an upward curving graph that starts shallow. Graph (3) shows this correctly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The color code of a resistance is given below (Yellow, Violet, Brown, Gold). The values of resistance and tolerance, respectively, are:
questions.explanation: Yellow = 4, Violet = 7, Brown = multiplier 10¹ = 10, Gold = ±5% tolerance. Resistance = 47 × 10 = 470 Ω, tolerance = 5%.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For the logic circuit shown, the truth table is:
questions.explanation: The circuit shows two NOT gates feeding into a NOR gate. A'NOR B' = NOT(A'+B') = A·B (AND gate). Truth table matches AND: output is 1 only when both A=1 and B=1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two bodies of mass 4 kg and 6 kg are tied to the ends of a massless string. The string passes over a frictionless pulley (see figure). The acceleration of the system in terms of acceleration due to gravity (g) is:
questions.explanation: For an Atwood machine: a = (m₂ − m₁)g / (m₁ + m₂) = (6 − 4)g / (6 + 4) = 2g/10 = g/5.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an oscillating spring mass system, a spring is connected to a box filled with sand. As the box oscillates, sand leaks slowly out of the box vertically so that the average frequency ω(t) and average amplitude A(t) of the system change with time t. Which one of the following options schematically depicts these changes correctly?
questions.explanation: As sand leaks out, mass m decreases. ω = √(k/m), so ω increases with time. Amplitude: energy is conserved as sand leaves (leaks vertically with zero horizontal velocity), so A = √(2E/k) stays constant or slightly increases. Option (3) shows ω increasing and A constant — this is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: AB is a part of an electrical circuit (see figure). The potential difference V_A − V_B, at the instant when current i = 2 A and is increasing at a rate of 1 amp/second is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bob of heavy mass m is suspended by a light string of length l. The bob is given a horizontal velocity v₀ as shown in figure. If the string gets slack at some point P making an angle θ from the horizontal, the ratio of the speed v of the bob at point P to its initial speed v₀ is:
questions.explanation: At point P, string is slack so T=0, meaning mg sinθ = mv²/l → v² = gl sinθ. Using energy conservation: ½mv₀² = ½mv² + mg l(1 + sinθ). Substituting v² = gl sinθ gives v/v₀ = √(sinθ/(2 + 3sinθ)).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The output (Y) of the given logic implementation is similar to the output of an/a _____ gate.
questions.explanation: The circuit shows: two OR gates feeding into a NAND gate with A as repeated input. Analysis shows Y = NOR(A,B). Output is 1 only when both A=0 and B=0, which is the NOR gate truth table.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A constant voltage of 50 V is maintained between the points A and B of the circuit shown in the figure. The current through the branch CD of the circuit is:
questions.explanation: The network has 1Ω and 2Ω in series (top branch = 3Ω) and 3Ω and 4Ω in series (bottom branch = 7Ω) connected in parallel between A and B. Using circuit analysis, the current through CD branch (between the two parallel paths) = 2.0 A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The current passing through the battery in the given circuit is:
questions.explanation: Solving the resistor network: the equivalent resistance between the battery terminals works out to 10 Ω. With V = 5V, current through battery = 5/10 = 0.5 A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a uniform magnetic field of 0.049 T, a magnetic needle performs 20 complete oscillations in 5 seconds as shown. The moment of inertia of the needle is 9.8 × 10⁻⁶ kg m². If the magnitude of magnetic moment of the needle is x × 10⁻⁵ Am², then the value of x is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capacitor of capacitance C₁ is charged to potential V volt and then connected in parallel to an uncharged capacitor of capacity C₂. The final potential difference across each capacitor will be
questions.explanation: Common potential = (C₁V + C₂×0)/(C₁ + C₂) = C₁V/(C₁ + C₂).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a parallel plate capacitor, the distance between the plates is d and potential difference across plates is V. Energy stored per unit volume between the plates of capacitor is
questions.explanation: Energy density u = ½ε₀E² = ½ε₀(V/d)² = ½ε₀V²/d².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capacitor is charged by connecting a battery across its plates. It stores energy U. Now the battery is disconnected and another identical capacitor is connected across it, then the energy stored by both capacitors of the system will be
questions.explanation: Initial energy U = q²/2C. After connecting identical capacitor, equivalent capacitance = 2C, charge q remains same. Final energy = q²/2(2C) = q²/4C = U/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the potential of a capacitor having capacity 6 μF is increased from 10 V to 20 V, then increase in its energy will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 4 μF capacitor is charged to 400 V and then its plates are joined through a resistance of 1 kΩ. The heat produced in the resistance is
questions.explanation: Heat produced = energy stored = ½CV² = ½ × 4 × 10⁻⁶ × (400)² = 2 × 10⁻⁶ × 160000 = 0.32 J.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three resistors having resistances r₁, r₂ and r₃ are connected as shown in the given circuit. The ratio i₃/i₁ of currents in terms of resistances used in the circuit is
questions.explanation: From parallel combination, i₂r₂ = i₃r₃ and i₁ = i₂ + i₃. Solving gives i₃/i₁ = r₂/(r₁ + r₂).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two solid conductors are made up of same material, have same length and same resistance. One of them has a circular cross-section of area A₁ and the other one has a square cross-section of area A₂. The ratio A₁/A₂ is
questions.explanation: R = ρL/A. Same material (ρ same), same length, same resistance ⇒ A same. So A₁/A₂ = 1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The effective resistance of a parallel connection that consists of four wires of equal length, equal area of cross-section and same material is 0.25 Ω. What will be the effective resistance if they are connected in series?
questions.explanation: Each wire resistance = R. For parallel: R/4 = 0.25 ⇒ R = 1 Ω. For series: 4R = 4 Ω.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A charged particle having drift velocity of 7.5 × 10⁻⁴ m/s in an electric field of 3 × 10⁻¹⁰ V/m has a mobility (in m²V⁻¹s⁻¹) of

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The color code of a resistance is given below. The values of resistance and tolerance respectively are
questions.explanation: Yellow = 4, Violet = 7, Brown = 10¹, Gold = ±5%. Resistance = 47 × 10¹ = 470 Ω, 5%.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following acts as a circuit protection device?
questions.explanation: Fuse protects circuit by melting when current exceeds limit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The resistance of a wire is R ohm. If it is melted and stretched to n times its original length, its new resistance will be
questions.explanation: Volume constant: A₁l₁ = A₂l₂ ⇒ A₂ = A₁/n. R₂ = ρl₂/A₂ = ρ(nl₁)/(A₁/n) = n²(ρl₁/A₁) = n²R.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire of resistance 4 Ω is stretched to twice its original length. The resistance of stretched wire would be
questions.explanation: R₂ = n²R = 2² × 4 = 16 Ω.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: n resistances each of r ohm, when connected in parallel give an equivalent resistance of R ohm. If these resistances were connected in series, the combination would have a resistance equal to
questions.explanation: For parallel: R = r/n ⇒ r = nR. For series: R_series = nr = n(nR) = n²R.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The electric resistance of a certain wire of iron is R. If its length and radius are both doubled, then
questions.explanation: R = ρl/A ∝ l/r². New l' = 2l, r' = 2r ⇒ R' ∝ (2l)/(4r²) = (1/2)(l/r²) = R/2. ρ depends on material only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The specific resistance of a conductor increases with
questions.explanation: Resistivity of metals increases with temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The masses of the three wires of copper are in the ratio 1:3:5 and their lengths are in the ratio 5:3:1. The ratio of their electrical resistance is
questions.explanation: Mass = density × volume = density × A × l ⇒ A ∝ m/l. R = ρl/A ∝ l²/m. So R₁:R₂:R₃ = (25/1):(9/3):(1/5) = 25:3:0.2 = 125:15:1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The charge flowing through a resistance R varies with time t as Q = at - bt², where a and b are positive constants. The total heat produced in R is
questions.explanation: I = dQ/dt = a - 2bt. Current exists till t = a/2b. H = ∫I²R dt = R∫₀^(a/2b) (a - 2bt)² dt = a²R/6b.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A filament bulb (500 W, 100 V) is to be used in a 230 V main supply. When a resistance R is connected in series, it works perfectly and the bulb consumes 500 W. The value of R is
questions.explanation: Bulb resistance R_b = V²/P = 100²/500 = 20 Ω. Current I = P/V = 500/100 = 5 A. Total R + R_b = 230/5 = 46 Ω ⇒ R = 26 Ω.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two cities are 150 km apart. Electric power is sent from one city to another city through copper wires. The fall of potential per km is 8 V and the average resistance per km is 0.5 Ω. The power loss in the wire is
questions.explanation: Total potential drop = 150 × 8 = 1200 V. Total resistance = 150 × 0.5 = 75 Ω. Power loss = V²/R = (1200)²/75 = 1440000/75 = 19200 W = 19.2 kW.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If voltage across a bulb rated 220 V-100 W drops by 2.5% of its rated value, the percentage of the rated value by which the power would decrease is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 5 A fuse wire can withstand a maximum power of 1 W in circuit. The resistance of the fuse wire is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In India, electricity is supplied for domestic use at 220 V. It is supplied at 110 V in USA. If the resistance of a 60 W bulb for use in India is R, the resistance of a 60 W bulb for use in USA will be
questions.explanation: P = V²/R = constant ⇒ R ∝ V². R_USA/R_India = (110/220)² = (1/2)² = 1/4 ⇒ R_USA = R/4.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two 220 V, 100 W bulbs are connected first in series and then in parallel. Each time the combination is connected to a 220 V AC supply line. The power drawn by the combination in each case respectively will be
questions.explanation: Each bulb resistance R = V²/P = 220²/100 = 484 Ω. Series: R_s = 2R, P_s = V²/2R = P/2 = 50 W. Parallel: R_p = R/2, P_p = V²/(R/2) = 2P = 200 W.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electric kettle has two heating coils. When one coil is used, water boils in 10 min. When the other coil is used, water boils in 40 min. If both coils are connected in parallel, the time taken will be
questions.explanation: H = V²t/R = constant. R₁/R₂ = t₁/t₂ = 10/40 = 1/4 ⇒ R₂ = 4R₁. Parallel: R_p = R₁R₂/(R₁+R₂) = (R₁×4R₁)/(5R₁) = 4R₁/5. t_p/t₁ = R_p/R₁ = 4/5 ⇒ t_p = (4/5)×10 = 8 min.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Fuse wire is a wire of

options.A: low resistance and low melting point
options.B: low resistance and high melting point
options.C: high resistance and high melting point
options.D: high resistance and low melting point

questions.explanation: Fuse wire should have high resistance to produce heat and low melting point to melt quickly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two bulbs 25 W, 220 V and 100 W, 220 V are given. Which has higher resistance?
questions.explanation: R = V²/P. For same V, R ∝ 1/P. So lower power bulb has higher resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 5°C rise in temperature is observed in a conductor by passing a current. When the current is doubled, the rise in temperature will be approximately
questions.explanation: Heat H ∝ I². Temperature rise ∝ H. So Δθ₂/Δθ₁ = (I₂/I₁)² = 4 ⇒ Δθ₂ = 4 × 5 = 20°C.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three equal resistors connected in series across a source of emf together dissipate 10 W of power. What will be the power dissipated if the same resistors are connected in parallel across the same source?
questions.explanation: Series: R_s = 3R, P_s = V²/3R = 10 ⇒ V²/R = 30. Parallel: R_p = R/3, P_p = V²/(R/3) = 3V²/R = 3 × 30 = 90 W.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 100 W, 200 V bulb is connected to a 160 V power supply. The power consumption would be
questions.explanation: R = V_rated²/P_rated = 200²/100 = 400 Ω. P_actual = V_actual²/R = 160²/400 = 25600/400 = 64 W.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A heating coil is labelled 100 W, 220 V. The coil is cut in two equal halves and the two pieces are joined in parallel to the same source. The energy now liberated per second is
questions.explanation: Original R = V²/P = 220²/100 = 484 Ω. Each half has R/2 = 242 Ω. Parallel combination: R_p = (R/2)/2 = R/4 = 121 Ω. New power P' = V²/R_p = 220²/121 = 48400/121 = 400 W = 400 J/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 4 μF conductor is charged to 400 V and then its plates are joined through a resistance of 1 kΩ. The heat produced in the resistance is
questions.explanation: Heat = energy stored = ½CV² = ½ × 4 × 10⁻⁶ × (400)² = 2 × 10⁻⁶ × 160000 = 0.32 J.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: 40 electric bulbs are connected in series across a 220 V supply. After one bulb is fused, the remaining 39 are connected again in series across the same supply. The illumination will be
questions.explanation: Resistance decreases when one bulb removed (39R < 40R). Current increases, so power increases. Illumination more with 39 bulbs.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A current of 2 A, passing through a conductor produces 80 J of heat in 10 s. The resistance of the conductor is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For the circuit shown in the figure, the current I will be
questions.explanation: Applying KVL: 4 + 1 - 4 + 1 - I - 2I = 0 ⇒ 2 - 3I = 0 ⇒ I = 2/3? Wait, careful: 4 + 1 - 4 + 1 = 2, then -I - 2I = -3I, so 2 - 3I = 0 ⇒ I = 2/3 A. But answer given is 1 A. Let me recalc: Actually the equation is 4 + 1 - 4 + 1 - I - 2I = 0 ⇒ 2 - 3I = 0 ⇒ I = 2/3 A. There's inconsistency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A set of 'n' equal resistors, of value R each, are connected in series to a battery of emf E and internal resistance R. The current drawn is I. Now, the 'n' resistors are connected in parallel to the same battery. Then, the current drawn from battery becomes 10I. The value of 'n' is
questions.explanation: Series: I = E/(nR + R) = E/R(n+1). Parallel: I' = E/(R/n + R) = nE/R(n+1) = 10I. Substituting I gives nE/R(n+1) = 10 × E/R(n+1) ⇒ n = 10.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A battery consists of a variable number 'n' of identical cells (having internal resistance r each) which are connected in series. The terminals of the battery are short-circuited and the current I is measured. Which of the following is true about I and n?
questions.explanation: I = ne/nr = e/r = constant, independent of n.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The internal resistance of a 2.1 V cell which gives a current of 0.2 A through a resistance of 10 Ω is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A current of 2 A flows through a 2 Ω resistor when connected across a battery. The same battery supplies a current of 0.5 A when connected across a 9 Ω resistor. The internal resistance of the battery is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Consider the following two statements: I. Kirchhoff's junction law follows from conservation of charge. II. Kirchhoff's loop law follows from conservation of energy. Which is correct?

options.A: Both I and II are wrong
options.B: I is correct and II is wrong
options.C: I is wrong and II is correct
options.D: Both I and II are correct

questions.explanation: Junction law - charge conservation. Loop law - energy conservation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A student measures the terminal potential difference V of a cell (of emf ε and internal resistance r) as a function of current I. The slope and intercept of the graph between V and I respectively equal to
questions.explanation: V = ε - Ir. Comparing with y = mx + c, slope = -r, intercept = ε.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two cells, having the same emf, are connected in series through an external resistance R. Cells have internal resistances r₁ and r₂ (r₁ > r₂) respectively. When the circuit is closed, the potential difference across the first cell is zero. The value of R is
questions.explanation: Current I = 2E/(R + r₁ + r₂). V₁ = E - Ir₁ = 0 ⇒ E = Ir₁ ⇒ I = E/r₁. Equating: E/r₁ = 2E/(R + r₁ + r₂) ⇒ R + r₁ + r₂ = 2r₁ ⇒ R = r₁ - r₂.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two batteries, one of emf 18 V and internal resistance 2 Ω and the other of emf 12 V and internal resistance 1 Ω, are connected as shown. The voltmeter V will record a reading of
questions.explanation: Net emf = 18 - 12 = 6 V, total resistance = 2 + 1 = 3 Ω. Current I = 6/3 = 2 A. V = E₁ - Ir₁ = 18 - 2×2 = 14 V.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 6 V battery is connected to the terminals of a 3 m long wire of uniform thickness and resistance of 100 Ω. The difference of potential between two points on the wire separated by a distance of 50 cm will be
questions.explanation: Current I = V/R = 6/100 = 0.06 A. Resistance of 50 cm = (50/300) × 100 = 50/3 Ω. Potential difference = I × R = 0.06 × (50/3) = 3/3 = 1 V.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A battery is charged at a potential of 15 V for 8 h when the current flowing is 10 A. The battery on discharge supplies a current of 5 A for 15 h. The mean terminal voltage during discharge is 14 V. The watt-hour efficiency of the battery is
questions.explanation: Input energy = 15 × 10 × 8 = 1200 Wh. Output energy = 14 × 5 × 15 = 1050 Wh. Efficiency = 1050/1200 × 100 = 87.5%. questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A cell has an emf 1.5 V. When connected across an external resistance of 2 Ω, the terminal potential difference falls to 1.0 V. The internal resistance of the cell is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The current in the following circuit is
questions.explanation: Equivalent resistance of parallel combination of 3 Ω and 6 Ω = 2 Ω. Total R = 2 Ω. I = V/R = 2/2 = 1 A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Kirchhoff's first law, i.e. Σi = 0 at a junction, deals with the conservation of
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's junction law follows from conservation of charge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the circuit of figure, the current in 4 Ω resistance is 1.2 A, what is the potential difference between B and C?
questions.explanation: Voltage across 4 Ω = 4 × 1.2 = 4.8 V. Current through 8 Ω = 4.8/8 = 0.6 A. Total current = 1.2 + 0.6 = 1.8 A. Voltage across 2 Ω = 1.8 × 2 = 3.6 V.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Current through 3 Ω resistor is 0.8 A, then potential drop through 4 Ω resistor is
questions.explanation: Voltage across 3 Ω = 3 × 0.8 = 2.4 V. Current through 6 Ω = 2.4/6 = 0.4 A. Total current = 0.8 + 0.4 = 1.2 A. Voltage across 4 Ω = 1.2 × 4 = 4.8 V.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Kirchhoff's first law of electricity follows

options.A: only law of conservation of energy
options.B: only law of conservation of charge
options.C: law of conservation of both energy and charge

questions.explanation: Junction law is based on charge conservation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A battery of emf 10 V and internal resistance 0.5 Ω is connected across a variable resistance R. The value of R for which the power delivered in it is maximum is given by
questions.explanation: Maximum power transfer occurs when load resistance equals internal resistance. So R = r = 0.5 Ω.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two identical batteries each of emf 2 V and internal resistance 1 Ω are available to produce heat in an external resistance. The maximum power that can be developed across R using these batteries is
questions.explanation: For maximum power, R should equal internal resistance. Batteries in series: E_eq = 4 V, r_eq = 2 Ω. Set R = 2 Ω. Power = I²R = (4/4)² × 2 = 1 × 2 = 2 W.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two batteries of emf 4 V and 8 V with internal resistance 1 Ω and 2 Ω are connected in a circuit with a resistance of 9 Ω as shown. The current and potential difference between the points P and Q are
questions.explanation: Applying KVL: -2I + 8 - 4 - I - 9I = 0 ⇒ 4 - 12I = 0 ⇒ I = 1/3 A. V_PQ = I × 9 = 3 V.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a potentiometer circuit, a cell of emf 1.5 V gives balance point at 36 cm length of wire. If another cell of emf 2.5 V replaces the first cell, then at what length of the wire, the balance point occurs?

questions.subject: Physics
